Troy Stribling
Feb. 25207

Derivative of logarithm

Consider
y ( x ) = a x = ( e ln ⁡ a ) x = e x ln ⁡ a y ( x ) = a x = e ln ⁡ a x = e x ln ⁡ a y(x)=a^(x)=(e^(ln a))^(x)=e^(x ln a)y(x)=a^{x}=\left(e^{\ln a}\right)^{x}=e^{x \ln a}y(x)=ax=(eln⁡a)x=exln⁡a
so
d y d x = d d x ( a x ) = d d x ( e x ln ⁡ a ) = ( ln ⁡ a ) e x ln ⁡ a = ( ln ⁡ a ) ( e ln ⁡ a ) x = ( ln ⁡ a ) a x d y d x = d d x a x = d d x e x ln ⁡ a = ( ln ⁡ a ) e x ln ⁡ a = ( ln ⁡ a ) e ln ⁡ a x = ( ln ⁡ a ) a x {:[(dy)/(dx)=(d)/(dx)(a^(x))=(d)/(dx)(e^(x ln a))],[=(ln a)e^(x ln a)=(ln a)(e^(ln a))x],[=(ln a)a^(x)]:}\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{d}{d x}\left(a^{x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x \ln a}\right) \\ & =(\ln a) e^{x \ln a}=(\ln a)\left(e^{\ln a}\right) x \\ & =(\ln a) a^{x} \end{aligned}dydx=ddx(ax)=ddx(exln⁡a)=(ln⁡a)exln⁡a=(ln⁡a)(eln⁡a)x=(ln⁡a)ax

Probability Sum Rule

Consider the events A A AAA and B B BBB


A ∪ B = A ∪ B = A uu B=A \cup B=A∪B= union of A A AAA and B B BBB and defines the event where either A A AAA or B B BBB occur.
A ∩ B = A ∩ B = A nn B=A \cap B=A∩B= Intersection of A A AAA and B B BBB, indicated by red in the Uenn Diagram, defines the event where A A AAA and B B BBB occur.
Let
P ( A ) = Probability that cuent A occurs P ( B ) = Probability that event B occurs P ( A ) =  Probability that cuent  A  occurs  P ( B ) =  Probability that event  B  occurs  {:[P(A)=" Probability that cuent "A],[" occurs "],[P(B)={:[" Probability that event "B],[" occurs "]:}]:}\begin{aligned} & P(A)=\text { Probability that cuent } A \\ & \text { occurs } \\ & P(B)=\begin{array}{c} \text { Probability that event } B \\ \text { occurs } \end{array} \end{aligned}P(A)= Probability that cuent A occurs P(B)= Probability that event B occurs 
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ∪ B ) = P(A uu B)=P(A \cup B)=P(A∪B)= Probability the event A A AAA or Bloccurs.
Indiceled be green in the venn Diagram

P ( A ∩ B ) = P ( A ∩ B ) = P(A nn B)=P(A \cap B)=P(A∩B)= Probability trat botn A A AAA and B B BBB occur. Indicated by red in the venn Diagram.

From inspection of the venn Diagram it can be seen that
P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) P ( A ∪ B ) = P ( A ) + P ( B ) − P ( A ∩ B ) P(A uu B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A nn B)P(A \cup B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)
Next consider
P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) P(x uu y uu z)P(x \cup y \cup z)P(x∪y∪z)
let
A = X and B = Y ∪ Z A = X  and  B = Y ∪ Z A=X quad" and "quad B=Y uu ZA=X \quad \text { and } \quad B=Y \cup ZA=X and B=Y∪Z
The sum rule gives
P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ∪ z ) ⋅ P [ x ∩ ( y ∪ z ) ] P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ∪ z ) ⋅ P [ x ∩ ( y ∪ z ) ] P(x uu y uu z)=P(x)+P(y uu z)*P[x nn(y uu z)]P(x \cup y \cup z)=P(x)+P(y \cup z) \cdot P[x \cap(y \cup z)]P(x∪y∪z)=P(x)+P(y∪z)⋅P[x∩(y∪z)]
= P ( x ) + P ( y ∪ z ) − P [ ( x ∩ y ) ∪ ( x ∩ z ) ] = P ( x ) + P ( y ∪ z ) − P [ ( x ∩ y ) ∪ ( x ∩ z ) ] =P(x)+P(y uu z)-P[(x nn y)uu(x nn z)]=P(x)+P(y \cup z)-P[(x \cap y) \cup(x \cap z)]=P(x)+P(y∪z)−P[(x∩y)∪(x∩z)]
Apply the sum rule again
P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) P(x uu y uu z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)P(x \cup y \cup z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)P(x∪y∪z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)
− P ( Y ∩ Z ) − { P ( X ∩ Y ) + P ( X ∩ Z ) − P ( Y ∩ Z ) − { P ( X ∩ Y ) + P ( X ∩ Z ) -P(Y nn Z)-{P(X nn Y)+P(X nn Z)-P(Y \cap Z)-\{P(X \cap Y)+P(X \cap Z)−P(Y∩Z)−{P(X∩Y)+P(X∩Z)
− P [ ( x ∩ y ) ∩ ( x ∩ z ) ] ξ − P [ ( x ∩ y ) ∩ ( x ∩ z ) ] ξ -P[(x nn y)nn(x nn z)]xi-P[(x \cap y) \cap(x \cap z)] \xi−P[(x∩y)∩(x∩z)]ξ
= P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) ⋅ P ( y ∩ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) ⋅ P ( y ∩ z ) =P(x)+P(y)+P(z)*P(y nn z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z) \cdot P(y \cap z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)⋅P(y∩z)
− P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) − P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) -P(x nn y)-P(x nn z)+P(x nn y nn z)-P(x \cap y)-P(x \cap z)+P(x \cap y \cap z)−P(x∩y)−P(x∩z)+P(x∩y∩z)
visually
P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ x ) − P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( y ∩ x ∩ z ) P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ x ) − P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( y ∩ x ∩ z ) {:[P(x uu y uu z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)],[-P(y nn z)-P(y nn x)-P(x nn z)],[+P(y nn x nn z)]:}\begin{aligned} & P(x \cup y \cup z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z) \\ & -P(y \cap z)-P(y \cap x)-P(x \cap z) \\ & +P(y \cap x \cap z) \end{aligned}P(x∪y∪z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)−P(y∩z)−P(y∩x)−P(x∩z)+P(y∩x∩z)
To see the pattern one more union is required
P ( S ∪ x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( S ) + P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) − P [ S ∩ ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) ] P ( S ∪ x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( S ) + P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) − P [ S ∩ ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) ] {:[P(S uu x uu y uu z)=P(S)+P(x uu y uu z)],[-P[S nn(x uu y uu z)]]:}\begin{aligned} & P(S \cup x \cup y \cup z)=P(S)+P(x \cup y \cup z) \\ & -P[S \cap(x \cup y \cup z)] \end{aligned}P(S∪x∪y∪z)=P(S)+P(x∪y∪z)−P[S∩(x∪y∪z)]
Now
P [ S ∩ ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) ] = P [ ( S ∩ x ) ∪ ( S ∩ y ) ∪ ( S ∩ z ) ] P [ S ∩ ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) ] = P [ ( S ∩ x ) ∪ ( S ∩ y ) ∪ ( S ∩ z ) ] {:[P[S nn(x uu y uu z)]],[=P[(S nn x)uu(S nn y)uu(S nn z)]]:}\begin{aligned} & P[S \cap(x \cup y \cup z)] \\ & =P[(S \cap x) \cup(S \cap y) \cup(S \cap z)] \end{aligned}P[S∩(x∪y∪z)]=P[(S∩x)∪(S∩y)∪(S∩z)]
using the triple union sum rule
P [ ( S ∩ x ) ∪ ( S ∩ y ) ∪ ( S ∩ z ) ] = P ( S ∩ x ) + P ( S ∩ y ) + P ( S ∩ z ) − P ( S ∩ x ∩ y ) − P ( S ∩ x ∩ z ) − P ( S ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( S ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) P [ ( S ∩ x ) ∪ ( S ∩ y ) ∪ ( S ∩ z ) ] = P ( S ∩ x ) + P ( S ∩ y ) + P ( S ∩ z ) − P ( S ∩ x ∩ y ) − P ( S ∩ x ∩ z ) − P ( S ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( S ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) {:[P[(S nn x)uu(S nn y)uu(S nn z)]],[=P(S nn x)+P(S nn y)+P(S nn z)],[-P(S nn x nn y)-P(S nn x nn z)],[-P(S nn y nn z)+P(S nn x nn y nn z)]:}\begin{aligned} P & {[(S \cap x) \cup(S \cap y) \cup(S \cap z)] } \\ = & P(S \cap x)+P(S \cap y)+P(S \cap z) \\ - & P(S \cap x \cap y)-P(S \cap x \cap z) \\ - & P(S \cap y \cap z)+P(S \cap x \cap y \cap z) \end{aligned}P[(S∩x)∪(S∩y)∪(S∩z)]=P(S∩x)+P(S∩y)+P(S∩z)−P(S∩x∩y)−P(S∩x∩z)−P(S∩y∩z)+P(S∩x∩y∩z)
and
P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) P ( x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) {:[P(x uu y uu z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)],[-P(x nn y)-P(x nn z)-P(y nn z)],[+P(x nn y nn z)]:}\begin{aligned} & P(x \cup y \cup z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z) \\ & -P(x \cap y)-P(x \cap z)-P(y \cap z) \\ & +P(x \cap y \cap z) \end{aligned}P(x∪y∪z)=P(x)+P(y)+P(z)−P(x∩y)−P(x∩z)−P(y∩z)+P(x∩y∩z)
Bringing things together gives
P ( s ∪ x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( s ) + [ P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) ] − [ P ( s ∩ x ) + P ( s ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ z ) − P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ) − P ( s ∩ x ∩ z ) − P ( s ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) ] = [ P ( s ) + P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) ] − [ P ( x ∩ y ) + P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ) + P ( s ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ z ) ] + [ P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ y ∩ z ) ] − P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) P ( s ∪ x ∪ y ∪ z ) = P ( s ) + [ P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) − P ( x ∩ y ) − P ( x ∩ z ) − P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) ] − [ P ( s ∩ x ) + P ( s ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ z ) − P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ) − P ( s ∩ x ∩ z ) − P ( s ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) ] = [ P ( s ) + P ( x ) + P ( y ) + P ( z ) ] − [ P ( x ∩ y ) + P ( x ∩ z ) + P ( y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ) + P ( s ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ z ) ] + [ P ( x ∩ y ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ) + P ( s ∩ x ∩ z ) + P ( s ∩ y ∩ z ) ] − P ( s ∩ x ∩ y ∩ z ) {:[P(s uu x uu y uu z)=P(s)+[P(x)+P(y)+P(z)],[-P(x nn y)-P(x nn z)-P(y nn z)],[+P(x nn y nn z)]-[P(s nn x)+P(s nn y)],[+P(s nn z)-P(s nn x nn y)-P(s nn x nn z)],[-P(s nn y nn z)+P(s nn x nn y nn z)]],[=[P(s)+P(x)+P(y)+P(z)]-[P(x nn y)],[+P(x nn z)+P(y nn z)+P(s nn x)+P(s nn y)],[+P(s nn z)]+[P(x nn y nn z)+P(s nn x nn y)],[+P(s nn x nn z)+P(s nn y nn z)]],[-P(s nn x nn y nn z)]:}\begin{aligned} & P(s \cup x \cup y \cup z)=P(s)+[P(x)+P(y)+P(z) \\ & -P(x \cap y)-P(x \cap z)-P(y \cap z) \\ & +P(x \cap y \cap z)]-[P(s \cap x)+P(s \cap y) \\ & +P(s \cap z)-P(s \cap x \cap y)-P(s \cap x \cap z) \\ & -P(s \cap y \cap z)+P(s \cap x \cap y \cap z)] \\ & =[P(s)+P(x)+P(y)+P(z)]-[P(x \cap y) \\ & +P(x \cap z)+P(y \cap z)+P(s \cap x)+P(s \cap y) \\ & +P(s \cap z)]+[P(x \cap y \cap z)+P(s \cap x \cap y) \\ & +P(s \cap x \cap z)+P(s \cap y \cap z)] \\ & -P(s \cap x \cap y \cap z) \end{aligned}P(s∪x∪y∪z)=P(s)+[P(x)+P(y)+P(z)−P(x∩y)−P(x∩z)−P(y∩z)+P(x∩y∩z)]−[P(s∩x)+P(s∩y)+P(s∩z)−P(s∩x∩y)−P(s∩x∩z)−P(s∩y∩z)+P(s∩x∩y∩z)]=[P(s)+P(x)+P(y)+P(z)]−[P(x∩y)+P(x∩z)+P(y∩z)+P(s∩x)+P(s∩y)+P(s∩z)]+[P(x∩y∩z)+P(s∩x∩y)+P(s∩x∩z)+P(s∩y∩z)]−P(s∩x∩y∩z)
Now for unions of n n nnn sets
P ( ⋃ i = 1 n X i ) P ⋃ i = 1 n   X i P(uuu_(i=1)^(n)X_(i))P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} X_{i}\right)P(⋃i=1nXi)
we have
P ( ⋃ i = 1 n x i ) = ∑ i = 1 n P ( x i ) − ∑ i = 1 n − 1 ∑ j = i + 1 n P ( x i ∩ x j ) + ∑ i = 1 n − 2 ∑ j = i + 1 n − 1 ∑ k = j + 1 n P ( x i ∩ x j ∩ x k ) + ∑ i = 1 n − 3 ∑ j = i + 1 n − 2 ∑ k = j + 1 n − 1 ∑ l = k + 1 n P ( x i ∩ x j ∩ x k ∩ x l ) + ⋯ + ( − 1 ) n + 1 P ( x 1 ∩ x 2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ x n ) P ⋃ i = 1 n   x i = ∑ i = 1 n   P x i − ∑ i = 1 n − 1   ∑ j = i + 1 n   P x i ∩ x j + ∑ i = 1 n − 2   ∑ j = i + 1 n − 1   ∑ k = j + 1 n   P x i ∩ x j ∩ x k + ∑ i = 1 n − 3   ∑ j = i + 1 n − 2   ∑ k = j + 1 n − 1   ∑ l = k + 1 n   P x i ∩ x j ∩ x k ∩ x l + ⋯ + ( − 1 ) n + 1 P x 1 ∩ x 2 ∩ ⋯ ∩ x n {:[P(uuu_(i=1)^(n)x_(i))=sum_(i=1)^(n)P(x_(i))-sum_(i=1)^(n-1)sum_(j=i+1)^(n)P(x_(i)nnx_(j))],[+sum_(i=1)^(n-2)sum_(j=i+1)^(n-1)sum_(k=j+1)^(n)P(x_(i)nnx_(j)nnx_(k))],[+sum_(i=1)^(n-3)sum_(j=i+1)^(n-2)sum_(k=j+1)^(n-1)sum_(l=k+1)^(n)P(x_(i)nnx_(j)nnx_(k)nnx_(l))],[+cdots+(-1)^(n+1)P(x_(1)nnx_(2)nn cdots nnx_(n))]:}\begin{aligned} & P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} P\left(x_{i}\right)-\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} P\left(x_{i} \cap x_{j}\right) \\ & +\sum_{i=1}^{n-2} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n-1} \sum_{k=j+1}^{n} P\left(x_{i} \cap x_{j} \cap x_{k}\right) \\ & +\sum_{i=1}^{n-3} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n-2} \sum_{k=j+1}^{n-1} \sum_{l=k+1}^{n} P\left(x_{i} \cap x_{j} \cap x_{k} \cap x_{l}\right) \\ & +\cdots+(-1)^{n+1} P\left(x_{1} \cap x_{2} \cap \cdots \cap x_{n}\right) \end{aligned}P(⋃i=1nxi)=∑i=1nP(xi)−∑i=1n−1∑j=i+1nP(xi∩xj)+∑i=1n−2∑j=i+1n−1∑k=j+1nP(xi∩xj∩xk)+∑i=1n−3∑j=i+1n−2∑k=j+1n−1∑l=k+1nP(xi∩xj∩xk∩xl)+⋯+(−1)n+1P(x1∩x2∩⋯∩xn)

Probability of Compliment

Consider the set A A AAA where A C ( Ω , F , P ) A C ( Ω , F , P ) AC(Omega,F,P)A C(\Omega, F, P)AC(Ω,F,P)
for the probability space
Now let A c A c A^(c)A^{c}Ac denote the complement of A A AAA then
A ∩ A C = ∅ A ∪ A C = Ω A ∩ A C = ∅ A ∪ A C = Ω {:[A nnA^(C)=O/],[A uuA^(C)=Omega]:}\begin{aligned} & A \cap A^{C}=\varnothing \\ & A \cup A^{C}=\Omega \end{aligned}A∩AC=∅A∪AC=Ω
and
P ( Ω ) = 1 P ( ϕ ) = 0 P ( Ω ) = 1 P ( ϕ ) = 0 P(Omega)=1quad P(phi)=0P(\Omega)=1 \quad P(\phi)=0P(Ω)=1P(ϕ)=0
so using the sum rule
P ( A ∪ A c ) = P ( A ) + P ( A c ) + P ( A ∩ A c ) ⇒ P ( Ω ) = P ( A ) + P ( A c ) + P ( ϕ ) ⇒ 1 = P ( A ) + P ( A c ) ⇒ P ( A c ) = 1 − P ( A ) P A ∪ A c = P ( A ) + P A c + P A ∩ A c ⇒ P ( Ω ) = P ( A ) + P A c + P ( ϕ ) ⇒ 1 = P ( A ) + P A c ⇒ P A c = 1 − P ( A ) {:[P(A uuA^(c))=P(A)+P(A^(c))+P(A nnA^(c))],[=>P(Omega)=P(A)+P(A^(c))+P(phi)],[=>quad1=P(A)+P(A^(c))],[=>P(A^(c))=1-P(A)]:}\begin{aligned} & P\left(A \cup A^{c}\right)=P(A)+P\left(A^{c}\right)+P\left(A \cap A^{c}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow P(\Omega)=P(A)+P\left(A^{c}\right)+P(\phi) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1=P(A)+P\left(A^{c}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow P\left(A^{c}\right)=1-P(A) \end{aligned}P(A∪Ac)=P(A)+P(Ac)+P(A∩Ac)⇒P(Ω)=P(A)+P(Ac)+P(ϕ)⇒1=P(A)+P(Ac)⇒P(Ac)=1−P(A)
Probability of Set Difference Consider two sets A A AAA and B B BBB

The difference of A A AAA and
A ∖ B A ∖ B A\\BA \backslash BA∖B

where
a ∈ A ∖ B ⇒ a ∈ A and a ∉ B a ∈ A ∖ B ⇒ a ∈ A  and  a ∉ B a in A\\B=>a in A" and "a!in Ba \in A \backslash B \Rightarrow a \in A \text { and } a \notin Ba∈A∖B⇒a∈A and a∉B
This is denoted by the green part of A A AAA in the diagram
By inspection of the Uenn diagram it is seen that
P ( A ∖ B ) = P ( A ) − P ( A ∩ B ) P ( A ∖ B ) = P ( A ) − P ( A ∩ B ) P(A\\B)=P(A)-P(A nn B)P(A \backslash B)=P(A)-P(A \cap B)P(A∖B)=P(A)−P(A∩B)
  • Show that
A ∖ B = A ∩ B C A ∖ B = A ∩ B C A\\B=A nnB^(C)A \backslash B=A \cap B^{C}A∖B=A∩BC
Let a ∈ A ∖ B ⇒ a ∈ A , a ∉ B a ∈ A ∖ B ⇒ a ∈ A , a ∉ B a in A\\B=>a in A,a!in Ba \in A \backslash B \Rightarrow a \in A, a \notin Ba∈A∖B⇒a∈A,a∉B but a ∉ B ⇒ a ∈ B c a ∉ B ⇒ a ∈ B c a!in B=>a inB^(c)a \notin B \Rightarrow a \in B^{c}a∉B⇒a∈Bc so, a ∈ A ∩ B c a ∈ A ∩ B c a in A nnB^(c)a \in A \cap B^{c}a∈A∩Bc and A B ⊆ A ∩ B A B ⊆ A ∩ B AB sube A nn BA B \subseteq A \cap BAB⊆A∩B.
Let a ∈ A ∩ B c ⇒ a ∈ A , a ∈ B c a ∈ A ∩ B c ⇒ a ∈ A , a ∈ B c a in A nnB^(c)=>a in A,a inB^(c)a \in A \cap B^{c} \Rightarrow a \in A, a \in B^{c}a∈A∩Bc⇒a∈A,a∈Bc so a ∈ B a ∈ B a in Ba \in Ba∈B and a ∈ A ∖ B a ∈ A ∖ B a in A\\Ba \in A \backslash Ba∈A∖B thus
A ∖ B = A ∩ B C A ∖ B = A ∩ B C A\\B=A nnB^(C)A \backslash B=A \cap B^{C}A∖B=A∩BC
  • Consider the set
B i = A i ∖ A 1 ∪ A 1 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 B i = A i ∖ A 1 ∪ A 1 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 B_(i)=A_(i)\\A_(1)uuA_(1)uu cdots uuA_(i-1)B_{i}=A_{i} \backslash A_{1} \cup A_{1} \cup \cdots \cup A_{i-1}Bi=Ai∖A1∪A1∪⋯∪Ai−1
Show that
i) B i ∩ B j = Φ i ≠ j B i ∩ B j = Φ i ≠ j B_(i)nnB_(j)=Phiquad i!=jB_{i} \cap B_{j}=\Phi \quad i \neq jBi∩Bj=Φi≠j
and
(i) ⋃ i = 1 n A i = ⋃ i = 1 n B i  (i)  ⋃ i = 1 n   A i = ⋃ i = 1 n   B i " (i) "uuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)=uuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)\text { (i) } \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}=\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i} (i) ⋃i=1nAi=⋃i=1nBi
Now
B i = A i ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 B i = A i ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 B_(i)=A_(i)\\A_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(i-1)B_{i}=A_{i} \backslash A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{i-1}Bi=Ai∖A1∪A2∪⋯∪Ai−1
if
b i ∈ B i b i ∈ B i b_(i)inB_(i)b_{i} \in B_{i}bi∈Bi
implies
b i ∈ A i b i ∈ A i b_(i)inA_(i)b_{i} \in A_{i}bi∈Ai
but
b i ∉ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 b i ∉ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 b_(i)!inA_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(i-1)b_{i} \notin A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{i-1}bi∉A1∪A2∪⋯∪Ai−1
δ 0 δ 0 delta0\delta 0δ0
b i ∉ A k ∀ k = 1 , 2 , … , i − 1 b i ∉ A k ∀ k = 1 , 2 , … , i − 1 b_(i)!inA_(k)AA k=1,2,dots,i-1b_{i} \notin A_{k} \forall k=1,2, \ldots, i-1bi∉Ak∀k=1,2,…,i−1
i) Consider
B i ∩ B j B i ∩ B j B_(i)nnB_(j)B_{i} \cap B_{j}Bi∩Bj
clearly if i = j B i ∩ B i = B i i = j B i ∩ B i = B i i=j quadB_(i)nnB_(i)=B_(i)i=j \quad B_{i} \cap B_{i}=B_{i}i=jBi∩Bi=Bi
Without loss of generality
i > j and b j ∈ B j i > j  and  b j ∈ B j i > j" and "b_(j)inB_(j)i>j \text { and } b_{j} \in B_{j}i>j and bj∈Bj
but
b j ∈ B i ⇒ b j ′ ∈ A j b j ∈ B i ⇒ b j ′ ∈ A j b_(j)inB_(i)=>b_(j)^(')inA_(j)b_{j} \in B_{i} \Rightarrow b_{j}^{\prime} \in A_{j}bj∈Bi⇒bj′∈Aj
and
b j ∉ A k ∀ k = 1 , 2 , … j − 1 b j ∉ A k ∀ k = 1 , 2 , … j − 1 b_(j)!inA_(k)quad AAquad k=1,2,dots j-1b_{j} \notin A_{k} \quad \forall \quad k=1,2, \ldots j-1bj∉Ak∀k=1,2,…j−1
since i > j i > j i > ji>ji>j.
b j ∈ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A j ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 b j ∈ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A j ∪ ⋯ ∪ A i − 1 b_(j)inA_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(j)uu cdots uuA_(i-1)b_{j} \in A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{j} \cup \cdots \cup A_{i-1}bj∈A1∪A2∪⋯∪Aj∪⋯∪Ai−1
So
b j ∉ A i ∖ A 1 , A 2 , … , A i − 1 ⇒ b j ∈ B i b j ∉ A i ∖ A 1 , A 2 , … , A i − 1 ⇒ b j ∈ B i {:[b_(j)!inA_(i)\\A_(1)","A_(2)","dots","A_(i-1)],[=>b_(j)inB_(i)]:}\begin{aligned} & b_{j} \notin A_{i} \backslash A_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{i-1} \\ & \Rightarrow b_{j} \in B_{i} \end{aligned}bj∉Ai∖A1,A2,…,Ai−1⇒bj∈Bi
thus
B i ∩ B j = ∅ ∀ i ≠ j B i ∩ B j = ∅ ∀ i ≠ j B_(i)nnB_(j)=O/quad AAquad i!=jB_{i} \cap B_{j}=\varnothing \quad \forall \quad i \neq jBi∩Bj=∅∀i≠j
ii) Snow
⋃ i = 1 n B i = ⋃ i = 1 n A i ⋃ i = 1 n   B i = ⋃ i = 1 n   A i uuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)=uuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}=\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}⋃i=1nBi=⋃i=1nAi
If
b ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n B i b ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n   B i b inuuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)b \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}b∈⋃i=1nBi
Since B k ∩ B j = ∅ ∀ i ≠ j B k ∩ B j = ∅ ∀ i ≠ j B_(k)nnB_(j)=O/quad AA i!=jB_{k} \cap B_{j}=\varnothing \quad \forall i \neq jBk∩Bj=∅∀i≠j
There is one B i B i B_(i)B_{i}Bi, call it B K B K B_(K)B_{K}BK such that
b ∈ B k b ∈ B k b inB_(k)b \in B_{k}b∈Bk
since
B k = A k ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 B k = A k ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 B_(k)=A_(k)\\A_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(k-1)B_{k}=A_{k} \backslash A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{k-1}Bk=Ak∖A1∪A2∪⋯∪Ak−1
it follows that
b k ∈ A k b k ∈ A k b_(k)inA_(k)b_{k} \in A_{k}bk∈Ak
thus
b k ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n A i b k ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n   A i b_(k)inuuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)b_{k} \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}bk∈⋃i=1nAi
so
⋃ i = 1 n B i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n A i ⋃ i = 1 n   B i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n   A i uuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)subeuuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i} \subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}⋃i=1nBi⊆⋃i=1nAi
Likewise if
a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n A i a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n   A i a inuuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)a \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}a∈⋃i=1nAi
There is at least one A i A i A_(i)A_{i}Ai that contains a. Let A k A k A_(k)A_{k}Ak be the first such A i A i A_(i)A_{i}Ai.
If a a aaa is contained in all or the first A i A i A_(i)A_{i}Ai
A 1 = B 1 A 1 = B 1 A_(1)=B_(1)A_{1}=B_{1}A1=B1
50
a ∈ B 1 a ∈ B 1 a inB_(1)a \in B_{1}a∈B1
and
a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n B i a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n   B i a inuuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)a \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}a∈⋃i=1nBi
If a ∉ A a ∉ A a!in Aa \notin Aa∉A, but occurs first in some other A k A k A_(k)A_{k}Ak
a ∈ A k a ∉ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 a ∈ A k a ∉ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 {:[a inA_(k)],[a!inA_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(k-1)]:}\begin{aligned} & a \in A_{k} \\ & a \notin A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{k-1} \end{aligned}a∈Aka∉A1∪A2∪⋯∪Ak−1
80
a ∈ B k = A k ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 a ∈ B k = A k ∖ A 1 ∪ A 2 ∪ ⋯ ∪ A k − 1 a inB_(k)=A_(k)\\A_(1)uuA_(2)uu cdots uuA_(k-1)a \in B_{k}=A_{k} \backslash A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \cdots \cup A_{k-1}a∈Bk=Ak∖A1∪A2∪⋯∪Ak−1
and
a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n B i a ∈ ⋃ i = 1 n   B i a inuuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)a \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}a∈⋃i=1nBi
it follows that
⋃ i = 1 n A i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n B i ⋃ i = 1 n   A i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n   B i uuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)subeuuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i} \subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i}⋃i=1nAi⊆⋃i=1nBi
but
⋃ i = 1 n B i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n A i ⋃ i = 1 n   B i ⊆ ⋃ i = 1 n   A i uuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(i)subeuuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{i} \subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}⋃i=1nBi⊆⋃i=1nAi
it follows that
⋃ i = 1 n B 1 = ⋃ i = 1 n A i ⋃ i = 1 n   B 1 = ⋃ i = 1 n   A i uuu_(i=1)^(n)B_(1)=uuu_(i=1)^(n)A_(i)\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_{1}=\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}⋃i=1nB1=⋃i=1nAi

Moment Cenerating Function

The moment generating function for a random variabe I I III is defined by,
M ( t ) = E ( e t X ¯ ) M ( t ) = E e t X ¯ M(t)=E(e^(t bar(X)))M(t)=E\left(e^{t \bar{X}}\right)M(t)=E(etX¯)
Moments of X X XXX can be found by taking derivatives of M(t) and letting t = 0 t = 0 t=0t=0t=0,
d n d t n M ( t ) = d n d t n E ( e t x ¯ ) = E ( x ¯ n e t x ¯ ) d n d t n M ( t ) = d n d t n E e t x ¯ = E x ¯ n e t x ¯ {:[(d^(n))/(dt^(n))M(t)=(d^(n))/(dt^(n))E(e^(t bar(x)))],[=E( bar(x)^(n)e^(t bar(x)))]:}\begin{aligned} \frac{d^{n}}{d t^{n}} M(t) & =\frac{d^{n}}{d t^{n}} E\left(e^{t \bar{x}}\right) \\ & =E\left(\bar{x}^{n} e^{t \bar{x}}\right) \end{aligned}dndtnM(t)=dndtnE(etx¯)=E(x¯netx¯)
and
d n d t n M ( t ) | t = 0 = E ( X n ) d n d t n M ( t ) t = 0 = E X n (d^(n))/(dt^(n))M(t)|_(t=0)=E(X^(n))\left.\frac{d^{n}}{d t^{n}} M(t)\right|_{t=0}=E\left(X^{n}\right)dndtnM(t)|t=0=E(Xn)

Central Limit Theorem

The Central Limit Theorem states that the sum of random variables
S n = X ¯ 1 + X ¯ 2 + ⋯ + X ¯ n n S n = X ¯ 1 + X ¯ 2 + ⋯ + X ¯ n n S_(n)=( bar(X)_(1)+ bar(X)_(2)+cdots+ bar(X)_(n))/(n)S_{n}=\frac{\bar{X}_{1}+\bar{X}_{2}+\cdots+\bar{X}_{n}}{n}Sn=X¯1+X¯2+⋯+X¯nn
approaches a unit normal distribution with random variable)
1 σ n ( ∑ i = 1 n x ¯ i − n μ ) 1 σ n ∑ i = 1 n   x ¯ i − n μ (1)/(sigmasqrtn)(sum_(i=1)^(n) bar(x)_(i)-n mu)\frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{n}}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{x}_{i}-n \mu\right)1σn(∑i=1nx¯i−nμ)
as n → ∞ n → ∞ n rarr oon \rightarrow \inftyn→∞ where μ μ mu\muμ is the mean and σ σ sigma\sigmaσ the standard deviation of
X ¯ = ∑ i = 1 n X ¯ i X ¯ = ∑ i = 1 n   X ¯ i bar(X)=sum_(i=1)^(n) bar(X)_(i)\bar{X}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \bar{X}_{i}X¯=∑i=1nX¯i

Convergence in the mean

Convergence in the mean is defined by
lim n → ∞ E [ ( X n − c ) 2 ] = 0 lim n → ∞   E X n − c 2 = 0 lim_(n rarr oo)E[(X_(n)-c)^(2)]=0\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} E\left[\left(X_{n}-c\right)^{2}\right]=0limn→∞E[(Xn−c)2]=0
where c c ccc is a constant Now
Var ( x n ) = E ( x n 2 ) − [ E ( x n ) ] 2 Var x n = E x n 2 − E x n 2 Var(x_(n))=E(x_(n)^(2))-[E(x_(n))]^(2)\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right)=E\left(x_{n}^{2}\right)-\left[E\left(x_{n}\right)\right]^{2}Var(xn)=E(xn2)−[E(xn)]2
So
E [ ( x n − c ) 2 ] = E [ x n 2 − 2 c x n + c 2 ] = E ( x n 2 ) − 2 c E ( x n ) + c 2 = Var ( x n ) + [ E ( x n ) ] 2 − 2 c E ( x n ) + c 2 E x n − c 2 = E x n 2 − 2 c x n + c 2 = E x n 2 − 2 c E x n + c 2 = Var x n + E x n 2 − 2 c E x n + c 2 {:[E[(x_(n)-c)^(2)]=E[x_(n)^(2)-2cx_(n)+c^(2)]],[=E(x_(n)^(2))-2cE(x_(n))+c^(2)],[=Var(x_(n))+[E(x_(n))]^(2)-2cE(x_(n))+c^(2)]:}\begin{aligned} & E\left[\left(x_{n}-c\right)^{2}\right]=E\left[x_{n}^{2}-2 c x_{n}+c^{2}\right] \\ & =E\left(x_{n}^{2}\right)-2 c E\left(x_{n}\right)+c^{2} \\ & =\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right)+\left[E\left(x_{n}\right)\right]^{2}-2 c E\left(x_{n}\right)+c^{2} \end{aligned}E[(xn−c)2]=E[xn2−2cxn+c2]=E(xn2)−2cE(xn)+c2=Var(xn)+[E(xn)]2−2cE(xn)+c2
If
E ( x n ) = C E x n = C E(x_(n))=CE\left(x_{n}\right)=CE(xn)=C
then
E [ ( x n − c ) 2 ] = Var ( x n ) + c 2 − 2 c 2 + c 2 = Var ( x n ) E x n − c 2 = Var x n + c 2 − 2 c 2 + c 2 = Var x n {:[E[(x_(n)-c)^(2)]=Var(x_(n))+c^(2)],[-2c^(2)+c^(2)=Var(x_(n))]:}\begin{aligned} E\left[\left(x_{n}-c\right)^{2}\right] & =\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right)+c^{2} \\ -2 c^{2}+c^{2} & =\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right) \end{aligned}E[(xn−c)2]=Var(xn)+c2−2c2+c2=Var(xn)
thus if
Var ( x n ) = 0 Var x n = 0 Var(x_(n))=0\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right)=0Var(xn)=0
In summary
lim n → ∞ E [ ( x n − c ) 2 ] = 0 lim n → ∞   E x n − c 2 = 0 lim_(n rarr oo)E[(x_(n)-c)^(2)]=0\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} E\left[\left(x_{n}-c\right)^{2}\right]=0limn→∞E[(xn−c)2]=0
if
E ( x n ) = c E x n = c E(x_(n))=cE\left(x_{n}\right)=cE(xn)=c
and
Var ( x n ) = 0 Var x n = 0 Var(x_(n))=0\operatorname{Var}\left(x_{n}\right)=0Var(xn)=0

Laplace's Methad

Laplace's Method is a method for approximating integrals of the form
∫ a b e M f ( x ) d x ∫ a b   e M f ( x ) d x int_(a)^(b)e^(Mf(x))dx\int_{a}^{b} e^{M f(x)} d x∫abeMf(x)dx
when M ≫ 1 M ≫ 1 M≫1M \gg 1M≫1 where it is
assumed that f ( x ) f ( x ) f(x)f(x)f(x) is at least twice differentiable and has a global maximum at x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0.
since f ( x ) f ( x ) f(x)f(x)f(x) has a global maximum at x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0
f ′ ( x 0 ) = 0 f ′ x 0 = 0 f^(')(x_(0))=0f^{\prime}\left(x_{0}\right)=0f′(x0)=0
and
f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) < 0 f ′ ′ x 0 < 0 f^('')(x_(0)) < 0f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)<0f′′(x0)<0
If f ( x ) f ( x ) f(x)f(x)f(x) is expanded in a Taylor series about x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0 gives
f ( x ) ≈ f ( x 0 ) + f ′ ( x 0 ) ( x − x 0 ) + 1 2 f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) ( x − x 0 ) 2 + 0 ( ( x − x 0 ) 3 ) f ( x ) ≈ f x 0 + f ′ x 0 x − x 0 + 1 2 f ′ ′ x 0 x − x 0 2 + 0 x − x 0 3 {:[f(x)~~f(x_(0))+f^(')(x_(0))(x-x_(0))],[+(1)/(2)f^('')(x_(0))(x-x_(0))^(2)+0((x-x_(0))^(3))]:}\begin{aligned} f(x) \approx & f\left(x_{0}\right)+f^{\prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\left(x-x_{0}\right) \\ & +\frac{1}{2} f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\left(x-x_{0}\right)^{2}+0\left(\left(x-x_{0}\right)^{3}\right) \end{aligned}f(x)≈f(x0)+f′(x0)(x−x0)+12f′′(x0)(x−x0)2+0((x−x0)3)
= f ( x 0 ) + 1 2 f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) ( x − x 0 ) 2 = f ( x 0 ) − 1 2 | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) | ( x − x 0 ) 2 = f x 0 + 1 2 f ′ ′ x 0 x − x 0 2 = f x 0 − 1 2 f ′ ′ x 0 x − x 0 2 {:[=f(x_(0))+(1)/(2)f^('')(x_(0))(x-x_(0))^(2)],[=f(x_(0))-(1)/(2)|f^('')(x_(0))|(x-x_(0))^(2)]:}\begin{aligned} & =f\left(x_{0}\right)+\frac{1}{2} f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\left(x-x_{0}\right)^{2} \\ & =f\left(x_{0}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left|f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right|\left(x-x_{0}\right)^{2} \end{aligned}=f(x0)+12f′′(x0)(x−x0)2=f(x0)−12|f′′(x0)|(x−x0)2
since it is assumed that
f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) < 0 f ′ ′ x 0 < 0 f^('')(x_(0)) < 0f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)<0f′′(x0)<0
It follows that for x x xxx near x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0
∫ a b e M f ( x ) d x ≈ ∫ a b e M [ f ( x 0 ) − 1 2 | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) ) ( x 0 − x ) 2 ] d x = e M f ( x 0 ) ∫ a b e − 1 2 M | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) | ( x 0 − x ) 2 d x ∫ a b   e M f ( x ) d x ≈ ∫ a b   e M f x 0 − 1 2 f ′ ′ x 0 x 0 − x 2 d x = e M f x 0 ∫ a b   e − 1 2 M f ′ ′ x 0 x 0 − x 2 d x {:[int_(a)^(b)e^(Mf(x))dx~~int_(a)^(b)e^({:M[f(x_(0))-(1)/(2)|f^('')(x_(0)))(x_(0)-x)^(2)])dx],[=e^(Mf(x_(0)))int_(a)^(b)e^(-(1)/(2)M|f^('')(x_(0))|(x_(0)-x)^(2))dx]:}\begin{aligned} & \int_{a}^{b} e^{M f(x)} d x \approx \int_{a}^{b} e^{\left.M\left[\left.f\left(x_{0}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \right\rvert\, f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right)\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{2}\right]} d x \\ & =e^{M f\left(x_{0}\right)} \int_{a}^{b} e^{-\frac{1}{2} M\left|f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right|\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{2}} d x \end{aligned}∫abeMf(x)dx≈∫abeM[f(x0)−12|f′′(x0))(x0−x)2]dx=eMf(x0)∫abe−12M|f′′(x0)|(x0−x)2dx
If M ≫ 1 M ≫ 1 M≫1M \gg 1M≫1 contributions to the integral will fall off quickly as x x xxx departs from values near x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0 resulting in a sharp peak.
It follows that for M ≫ 1 M ≫ 1 M≫1M \gg 1M≫1
e μ f ( x ) ≈ e μ f ( x 0 ) e − 1 ∂ μ ( f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) ( x 0 − x ) 2 e μ f ( x ) ≈ e μ f x 0 e − 1 ∂ μ f ′ ′ x 0 x 0 − x 2 e^(mu f(x))~~e^(mu f(x_(0)))e^(-(1)/(del)mu(f^('')(x_(0))(x_(0)-x)^(2):})e^{\mu f(x)} \approx e^{\mu f\left(x_{0}\right)} e^{-\frac{1}{\partial} \mu\left(f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{2}\right.}eμf(x)≈eμf(x0)e−1∂μ(f′′(x0)(x0−x)2
Thus e μ f ( x ) e μ f ( x ) e^(mu f(x))e^{\mu f(x)}eμf(x) appraches a gaussion with scale paramefer
σ 2 = 1 M ( f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) ) σ 2 = 1 M f ′ ′ x 0 sigma^(2)=(1)/(M(f^('')(x_(0))))\sigma^{2}=\frac{1}{M\left(f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right)}σ2=1M(f′′(x0))
Laplace's Method states that for MSS I
∫ a b e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ 2 π μ | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) | e μ f ( x 0 ) ∫ a b   e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ 2 π μ f ′ ′ x 0 e μ f x 0 int_(a)^(b)e^(mu f(x))dx~~sqrt((2pi)/(mu|f^('')(x_(0))|))e^(mu f(x_(0)))\int_{a}^{b} e^{\mu f(x)} d x \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi}{\mu\left|f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right|}} e^{\mu f\left(x_{0}\right)}∫abeμf(x)dx≈2πμ|f′′(x0)|eμf(x0)
This result can be obtained from the previous result by utilizing the knowledge that for M ≫ 1 e M f ( x ) M ≫ 1 e M f ( x ) M≫1e^(Mf(x))M \gg 1 e^{M f(x)}M≫1eMf(x) becomes shardly peaked at x = x 0 x = x 0 x=x_(0)x=x_{0}x=x0. It follows that contributions to the integral will be dominated by x x xxx near x 0 x 0 x_(0)x_{0}x0 so the integration limits can be taken to ± ∞ ± ∞ +-oo\pm \infty±∞ thus
∫ a b e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ ∫ − ∞ ∞ e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ e μ f ( x 0 ) ∫ − ∞ ∞ e − 1 2 μ | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) | ( x 0 − x ) 2 d x = e μ f ( x 0 ) 2 π μ | f ′ ′ ( x 0 ) | ∫ a b   e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ ∫ − ∞ ∞   e μ f ( x ) d x ≈ e μ f x 0 ∫ − ∞ ∞   e − 1 2 μ f ′ ′ x 0 x 0 − x 2 d x = e μ f x 0 2 π μ f ′ ′ x 0 {:[int_(a)^(b)e^(mu f(x))dx~~int_(-oo)^(oo)e^(mu f(x))dx],[~~e^(mu f(x_(0)))int_(-oo)^(oo)e^(-(1)/(2)mu|f^('')(x_(0))|(x_(0)-x)^(2))dx],[=e^(mu f(x_(0)))sqrt((2pi)/(mu|f^('')(x_(0))|))]:}\begin{aligned} & \int_{a}^{b} e^{\mu f(x)} d x \approx \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{\mu f(x)} d x \\ \approx & e^{\mu f\left(x_{0}\right)} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{1}{2} \mu\left|f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right|\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{2}} d x \\ = & e^{\mu f\left(x_{0}\right)} \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi}{\mu\left|f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{0}\right)\right|}} \end{aligned}∫abeμf(x)dx≈∫−∞∞eμf(x)dx≈eμf(x0)∫−∞∞e−12μ|f′′(x0)|(x0−x)2dx=eμf(x0)2πμ|f′′(x0)|

Examples

Consider the sin ⁡ c ( x ) sin ⁡ c ( x ) sin c(x)\sin c(x)sin⁡c(x) function
sin ⁡ c ( x ) = sin ⁡ ( x ) x sin ⁡ c ( x ) = sin ⁡ ( x ) x sin c(x)=(sin(x))/(x)\sin c(x)=\frac{\sin (x)}{x}sin⁡c(x)=sin⁡(x)x
Laplace's Method will be used to approximate
e M sin ⁡ c ( x ) e M sin ⁡ c ( x ) e^(M sin c(x))e^{M \sin c(x)}eMsin⁡c(x)
for μ ≫ 1 μ ≫ 1 quad mu≫1\quad \mu \gg 1μ≫1.
x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0 is a global maximum of sin ⁡ c ( x ) sin ⁡ c ( x ) sin c(x)\sin c(x)sin⁡c(x)
Now at x = 0 sin ⁡ c ( x ) x = 0 sin ⁡ c ( x ) x=0sin c(x)x=0 \sin c(x)x=0sin⁡c(x) is undefined the limit can be evaluate using L'tlopitals rule
lim x → 0 sin ⁡ ( x ) = lim x → 0 cos ⁡ ( x ) = 1 lim x → 0   sin ⁡ ( x ) = lim x → 0   cos ⁡ ( x ) = 1 lim_(x rarr0)sin(x)=lim_(x rarr0)cos(x)=1\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin (x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \cos (x)=1limx→0sin⁡(x)=limx→0cos⁡(x)=1
To show that x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0 is a global maximum it must be shown that
lim x → 0 sin ⁡ c ′ ( x ) = 0 lim x → 0   sin ⁡ c ′ ( x ) = 0 lim_(x rarr0)sin c^(')(x)=0\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin c^{\prime}(x)=0limx→0sin⁡c′(x)=0
and that
lim x → 0 sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) < 0 lim x → 0   sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) < 0 lim_(x rarr0)sin c^('')(x) < 0\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin c^{\prime \prime}(x)<0limx→0sin⁡c′′(x)<0
also
lim x → 0 sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) lim x → 0   sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) lim_(x rarr0)sin c^('')(x)\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin c^{\prime \prime}(x)limx→0sin⁡c′′(x)
is needed to evaluate the scale parameter in the approximation obtained using Laplace's Method. To evaluate the limits consider
the Taylor series for sin ⁡ ( x ) sin ⁡ ( x ) sin(x)\sin (x)sin⁡(x) expanded about x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0 is gruen by
sin ⁡ ( x ) = ∑ k = 0 ∞ ( − 1 ) k ( 2 k + 1 ) ! x 2 k + 1 sin ⁡ ( x ) = ∑ k = 0 ∞   ( − 1 ) k ( 2 k + 1 ) ! x 2 k + 1 sin(x)=sum_(k=0)^(oo)((-1)^(k))/((2k+1)!)x^(2k+1)\sin (x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{k}}{(2 k+1)!} x^{2 k+1}sin⁡(x)=∑k=0∞(−1)k(2k+1)!x2k+1
It follows that
sin ⁡ c ( x ) = sin ⁡ ( x ) x = ∑ k = 0 ∞ ( − 1 ) k ( 2 k + 1 ) ! x 2 k = 1 − 1 3 ! x 2 + 1 5 ! x 4 − 1 9 ! x 6 + ⋯ sin ⁡ c ( x ) = sin ⁡ ( x ) x = ∑ k = 0 ∞   ( − 1 ) k ( 2 k + 1 ) ! x 2 k = 1 − 1 3 ! x 2 + 1 5 ! x 4 − 1 9 ! x 6 + ⋯ {:[sin c(x)=(sin(x))/(x)=sum_(k=0)^(oo)((-1)^(k))/((2k+1)!)x^(2k)],[=1-(1)/(3!)x^(2)+(1)/(5!)x^(4)-(1)/(9!)x^(6)+cdots]:}\begin{aligned} & \sin c(x)=\frac{\sin (x)}{x}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{k}}{(2 k+1)!} x^{2 k} \\ & =1-\frac{1}{3!} x^{2}+\frac{1}{5!} x^{4}-\frac{1}{9!} x^{6}+\cdots \end{aligned}sin⁡c(x)=sin⁡(x)x=∑k=0∞(−1)k(2k+1)!x2k=1−13!x2+15!x4−19!x6+⋯
Now the derivatives are given by
sin ⁡ c ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! x + 4 5 ! x 3 − 6 9 ! x 5 sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! + ( 4 ) ( 3 ) 5 ! x 2 − ( 6 ) ( 5 ) 9 ! x 4 sin ⁡ c ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! x + 4 5 ! x 3 − 6 9 ! x 5 sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! + ( 4 ) ( 3 ) 5 ! x 2 − ( 6 ) ( 5 ) 9 ! x 4 {:[sin c^(')(x)=-(2)/(3!)x+(4)/(5!)x^(3)-(6)/(9!)x^(5)],[sin c^('')(x)=-(2)/(3!)+((4)(3))/(5!)x^(2)-((6)(5))/(9!)x^(4)]:}\begin{aligned} & \sin c^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{2}{3!} x+\frac{4}{5!} x^{3}-\frac{6}{9!} x^{5} \\ & \sin c^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{2}{3!}+\frac{(4)(3)}{5!} x^{2}-\frac{(6)(5)}{9!} x^{4} \end{aligned}sin⁡c′(x)=−23!x+45!x3−69!x5sin⁡c′′(x)=−23!+(4)(3)5!x2−(6)(5)9!x4
so
lim x → 0 sin ′ ⁡ ( x ) = 0 lim x → 0   sin ′ ⁡ ( x ) = 0 lim_(x rarr0)sin^(')(x)=0\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin ^{\prime}(x)=0limx→0sin′⁡(x)=0
and
lim x → 0 sinc ′ ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! = − 2 6 = − 1 3 lim x → 0   sinc ′ ′ ( x ) = − 2 3 ! = − 2 6 = − 1 3 lim_(x rarr0)sinc^('')(x)=-(2)/(3!)=-(2)/(6)=-(1)/(3)\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{sinc}^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{2}{3!}=-\frac{2}{6}=-\frac{1}{3}limx→0sinc′′(x)=−23!=−26=−13
Thus x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0 is an extremum since
lim x → 0 sinc ′ ( x ) = 0 lim x → 0   sinc ′ ( x ) = 0 lim_(x rarr0)sinc^(')(x)=0\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \operatorname{sinc}^{\prime}(x)=0limx→0sinc′(x)=0
and a maximum since
lim x → 0 sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) = − 1 3 < 0 lim x → 0   sin ⁡ c ′ ′ ( x ) = − 1 3 < 0 lim_(x rarr0)sin c^('')(x)=-(1)/(3) < 0\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin c^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{3}<0limx→0sin⁡c′′(x)=−13<0
It follows that the laplace method approximation to
e M sin ⁡ c ( x ) e M sin ⁡ c ( x ) e^(M sin c(x))e^{M \sin c(x)}eMsin⁡c(x)
for M ≫ 1 M ≫ 1 M≫1M \gg 1M≫1
e μ sin ⁡ c ( x ) ≈ e μ e − μ 6 x 2 e μ sin ⁡ c ( x ) ≈ e μ e − μ 6 x 2 e^(mu sin c(x))~~e^(mu)e^(-(mu)/(6)x^(2))e^{\mu \sin c(x)} \approx e^{\mu} e^{-\frac{\mu}{6} x^{2}}eμsin⁡c(x)≈eμe−μ6x2
lim n → ∞ ( A n ) n B lim n → ∞   A n n B lim_(n rarr oo)((A)/(n))^((n)/(B))\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{A}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{B}}limn→∞(An)nB
Consider the function
f ( n ) = ( A n ) n B f ( n ) = A n n B f(n)=((A)/(n))^((n)/(B))f(n)=\left(\frac{A}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{B}}f(n)=(An)nB
where A A AAA and B B BBB are constant. Now consider
f ( n ) = e ln ⁡ f ( n ) f ( n ) = e ln ⁡ f ( n ) f(n)=e^(ln f(n))f(n)=e^{\ln f(n)}f(n)=eln⁡f(n)
so first consider
lim n → ∞ ln ⁡ f ( n ) = lim n → ∞ ln ⁡ [ ( A n ) n B ] = lim n → ∞ n B ln ⁡ ( A n ) = lim n → ∞ n B ( ln ⁡ A − ln ⁡ n ) = − ∞ lim n → ∞   ln ⁡ f ( n ) = lim n → ∞   ln ⁡ A n n B = lim n → ∞   n B ln ⁡ A n = lim n → ∞   n B ( ln ⁡ A − ln ⁡ n ) = − ∞ {:[lim_(n rarr oo)ln f(n)=lim_(n rarr oo)ln[((A)/(n))^((n)/(B))]],[=lim_(n rarr oo)(n)/(B)ln((A)/(n))],[=lim_(n rarr oo)(n)/(B)(ln A-ln n)],[=-oo]:}\begin{aligned} & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \ln f(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \ln \left[\left(\frac{A}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{B}}\right] \\ = & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{n}{B} \ln \left(\frac{A}{n}\right) \\ = & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{n}{B}(\ln A-\ln n) \\ = & -\infty \end{aligned}limn→∞ln⁡f(n)=limn→∞ln⁡[(An)nB]=limn→∞nBln⁡(An)=limn→∞nB(ln⁡A−ln⁡n)=−∞
Thus
lim n → ∞ f ( n ) = lim n → ∞ e ln ⁡ f ( n ) = 0 lim n → ∞   f ( n ) = lim n → ∞   e ln ⁡ f ( n ) = 0 lim_(n rarr oo)f(n)=lim_(n rarr oo)e^(ln f(n))=0\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} f(n)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} e^{\ln f(n)}=0limn→∞f(n)=limn→∞eln⁡f(n)=0
So
lim n → ∞ ( A n ) n = 0 lim n → ∞   A n n = 0 lim_(n rarr oo)((A)/(n))^(n)=0\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{A}{n}\right)^{n}=0limn→∞(An)n=0
